coredata.dc:title: Synthesis and drug release kinetics of ciprofloxacin from polyacrylamide/dextran/carbon quantum dots (PAM/Dex/CQD) hydrogels
coredata.dc:creator: Sahu, Krishna Manjari
Biswal, Anuradha
Manisha, Upuluri
Swain, Sarat K.
coredata.dc:description: Sustainable release of drug by utilizing β-cyclodextrin (β-CD) based inclusion complex (IC) is the prime objective of the present work. Herein, polyacrylamide/dextran containing carbon quantum dots (PAM/Dex/CQD) nanocomposite hydrogels are prepared by in situ polymerization of acrylamide. The incorporation of CQD triggers the change in orientation of the PAM/Dex polymeric chains to result the formation of stacked surface morphology of the hydrogel. The average particle size of CQD is found to be 4.13 nm from HRTEM analysis. As-synthesized nanocomposite hydrogel exhibits an optimum swelling ratio of 863 % in aqueous medium. The cytotoxicity study is conducted on HeLa cells by taking up to 2 μM concentration of the prepared nanocomposite hydrogel demonstrate 78 % cell viability. In present study, ciprofloxacin (Cipro) is taken as model drug that achieves release of 64.15 % in 32 h from β-Cipro@PAM/Dex/CQD hydrogels in acidic medium. From theoretical study, release rate constants, R2, Akaike information criterion (AIC) and model selection criterion (MSC) are computed to determine the best fitted kinetics model. Peppas-Sahlin model is the best fitted kinetics model for β-Cipro@PAM/Dex/CQD and concluded that the release of Cipro follows Fickian drug diffusion mechanism in acidic medium.

head.ce:title: Synthesis and drug release kinetics of ciprofloxacin from polyacrylamide/dextran/carbon quantum dots (PAM/Dex/CQD) hydrogels

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Veer Surendra Sai University of Technology, Burla, Sambalpur 768018, Odisha, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Veer Surendra Sai University of Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Veer Surendra Sai University of Technology, Burla, Sambalpur, 768018, Odisha, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Sustainable release of drug by utilizing β-cyclodextrin (β-CD) based inclusion complex (IC) is the prime objective of the present work. Herein, polyacrylamide/dextran containing carbon quantum dots (PAM/Dex/CQD) nanocomposite hydrogels are prepared by in situ polymerization of acrylamide. The incorporation of CQD triggers the change in orientation of the PAM/Dex polymeric chains to result the formation of stacked surface morphology of the hydrogel. The average particle size of CQD is found to be 4.13 nm from HRTEM analysis. As-synthesized nanocomposite hydrogel exhibits an optimum swelling ratio of 863 % in aqueous medium. The cytotoxicity study is conducted on HeLa cells by taking up to 2 μM concentration of the prepared nanocomposite hydrogel demonstrate 78 % cell viability. In present study, ciprofloxacin (Cipro) is taken as model drug that achieves release of 64.15 % in 32 h from β-Cipro@PAM/Dex/CQD hydrogels in acidic medium. From theoretical study, release rate constants, R2, Akaike information criterion (AIC) and model selection criterion (MSC) are computed to determine the best fitted kinetics model. Peppas-Sahlin model is the best fitted kinetics model for β-Cipro@PAM/Dex/CQD and concluded that the release of Cipro follows Fickian drug diffusion mechanism in acidic medium.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Designing and development of advanced drug administration vehicle to resolve health care and medical related challenges continue to attract the attention of researchers during last few years [1]. Drug administration vehicles featured with sustainable, controlled and site-specific deliverable functionalities are the prime requirement in field of drug delivery to achieve high and desired therapeutic effects [2]. Hydrogels are the most adequate candidates to act as advanced therapeutic delivery carriers as they exhibit the propensity of drug administration through guarded and justifiable path [3]. Drug delivery systems based on hydrogel exhibit and cardinal distinct merits including easy tunable ability to modulate drug release rate and less interaction with encapsulated components which enables faster release of drug and ability to protect therapeutics from outer environment [4]. Rapid responsive nature of hydrogel towards external factors such as alteration in pH [5], light [6], temperature [7], magnetic field [8], electric field [9], etc., provides suitable circumambient for controlled drug release and helps in releasing requisite quantity of therapeutics at accurate overarching extravagance inside the body [4].

Selection of polymer for the engineering process of hydrogel-based drug carriers is an extremely pivotal step as the resultant characteristics of a particular material are the core sources to determine its viability [10,11]. Out of different polymers, polysaccharides are the key elements for the construction of functional drug administration vehicles owing to its biodegradable, biocompatible and reproducible nature [12]. Among polysaccharide, dextran (Dex) is the often-sought-after polysaccharide in biomedical applications [13]. It is a naturally occurring polysaccharide that comprises of glucose chains which are connected mainly by α-(1 → 6) glycosidic linkages [14]. Physiochemical properties of Dex assist during the formation of bioconjugate and nanogels [15]. Also, Dex is a renewable biological macromolecule that exhibits both biocompatible and biodegradable characteristics. Therefore, the present macromolecule Dex and its derivatives are extensively utilized as functional materials to fabricate drug administration vehicles [16]. Further, hydrogel made from Dex has the substantial drug encapsulation ability via the formation of hydrogen bonding because of its colloidal and hydrophilic properties [17] and are employed as therapeutic administration carriers for diverse range of therapeutic agents like proteins [18] and anticancer drugs [19]. Apart from hydrogel derived from natural polymers, synthetic polymers-based hydrogels are also vastly implemented in drug administration process [20]. One of such examples of polymer is polyacrylamide (PAM). Hydrogels of PAM are non toxic, biodegradable and has capability to undergo volume transition during swelling process [21]. Control of water content can restrict the drug release time, thus by varying the gelation ratio more denser PAM hydrogel can be made and consequently alteration can be done in drug delivery time. In addition to biocompatible characteristics, PAM is non-reactive towards all deliverable drugs. Hence, it can be acting as promising component in drug delivery vehicles [22,23]. However, pertinence of pristine hydrogel is not able to meet the standard of current medical trial due to increase in need and demand of accurate and sustainable release of drugs for treatment of patients with minimum acute side effects and high efficacy rate [24]. Therefore, researchers have focused more on investigation and implementation of alternate approaches like nanotechnology in drug administration application to ensure more sustainable release of the loaded pharmaceutical agents [25,26].

Nanotechnology is the proficient advancement in material science that helps to develop advanced drug delivery vehicles by accommodating fascinating properties of nanomaterials [27]. Polymeric nanocomposite (PNC) hydrogels have emerged as efficient biocompatible platform in various areas of biomedical field starting from sensing to biomedicine [28,29]. Polymers as well as nanoparticles are the salient components of PNC hydrogel that decides the viability and appropriateness of hydrogel in specific application [30]. To fabricate suitable PNC candidate for drug administration process, carbon quantum dot (CQD) is one of the favorable nanoparticles [31]. CQD is a significant class of carbonaceous nanomaterial of quasi spherical configuration and has diameter smaller than 10 nm [32]. CQD prepared from natural resources such as fruits, vegetables, leaves etc., is the most exploited carbon dot in biomedical field [33]. Biocompatibility, low-cost value, less toxic nature, higher water-soluble ability and most importantly its optical properties [34] are suitable for its employment in varieties of applications like photocatalysis [35], anticancer [36], wound dressing [37] and bactericidal activity [38]. In addition to this, prolonged circulation performance and outstanding passive targeting effect of CQD serve as efficacious properties for therapeutic delivery application [39]. Moreover, CQD has been incorporated in the preparation of various therapeutic delivery vehicles including polymeric nanocomposite platforms [40,41].

Ciprofloxacin (Cipro), also known as Siloxane Cipro Neofloxin is an eminent broad-spectrum antibiotic drug [42]. It comes under fluoroquinolone family of chemicals [43] and its solubility property is highly relying on the pH of the medium. This antibiotic drug is generally used to cure respiratory, digestive and urinary system. However, high and repetitive dosages of Cipro's administration involve adverse side effects such as vision loss, acute pancreatitis, hepatitis and so on. Hence, requisite amount of Cipro consumption and controlled release are the essential need to avoid complications [44]. For this purpose, researchers have introduced diverse varieties of materials to enable sustained and prolonged delivery. In recent time, it is reported that among sundry kinds of materials, cyclodextrin (CD) is the most judicious material for improving solubility and bioavailability of poorly soluble drugs [45]. Hydrophilic exterior surface and hydrophobic cavity of CD provide suitable environment to construct inclusion complex (IC) with distinct range of guest molecules. Therefore, these are widely implemented in pharmaceutical industry with an aim to enhance solubility and stability of medication, prolonged administration of drug and most importantly permeability of drug via biological barriers [46]. Among different CD, β-CD has drawn the attention of pharmaceutical researchers owing to its preferred chemical structure, low price, ease availability, degradable nature as well as its being non toxic towards human body [47]. In literatures, it is mentioned that β-CD is widely utilized to form IC with Cipro. This formation of IC helps in effective release of Cipro due to increment in solubility of the drug [48]. Furthermore, therapeutic delivery vehicles prepared from β-CD, natural or synthetic polymer have been extensively utilized in therapeutic administration process [49,50].

Although several works based on individual dextran, PAM and CQD have been reported in drug delivery application; however, their unique combination along with the use β-CD as supramolecular host molecule is not yet reported. The prime hypothesis of the present work is the construction of suitable drug carriers using β-CD mediated IC for controlled release of Cipro via host-guest mechanism. We aim at fabricating β-Cipro loaded CQD incorporated PAM/Dex (β-Cipro@PAM/Dex/CQD) nanocomposite hydrogel to study its impacts towards sustained release of Cipro in stomach pH to ensure delayed bioavailability of hydrophobic drugs. The CQD is synthesized from tulsi (Ocimum tenuiflorum) leaves or holy basil via simple carbonization method. PAM/Dex/CQD hydrogel shows better water intake and water retention ability as compared to pristine PAM/Dex hydrogel. The presence of IC elevates the Cipro uptake capacity of the overall hydrogel as compared to PAM/Dex/CQD which quantifies β-Cipro@PAM/Dex/CQD nanocomposite hydrogel to be a promising drug delivery vehicle. The experimental methods for drugs release mechanism are established through theoretical analysis of kinetics of drug release profiles.

Tulsi leaves were cumulated from the University campus to prepare CQD. Monomer acrylamide (AM) (MW = 71.08 g/mol) was procured from Sigma Aldrich. Dex with average MW of 10,000 g/mol was obtained from HiMedia Laboratories Pvt. Ltd. Mumbai, India. The host supramolecule, β-CD (MW = 1134.98 g/mol) was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Chemicals Pvt. Ltd. Bangalore, India. Ciprofloxacin; model drug for the present experiment with MW of 331.34 g/mol was supplied by Sigma Aldrich. Initiator potassium persulfate (KPS) and cross-linker N, N′-methylene bisacrylamide (MBA) having MW of 270.21 g/mol and 154.17 g/mol, respectively were obtained from Central Drug House (P) Ltd. Corp. New Delhi, India. All of these materials employed in this experiment were of analytical grade. Therefore, materials were directly utilized without any kind of purification process. Double distilled water was exploited to prepare the solutions and was implemented throughout experimental processes starting form preparation of CQD to drug delivery process.

Preparation procedure of CQD from tulsi leaves was carried out using carbonization process. At first, fresh tusli leaves were collected and were cleansed thoroughly with double distilled water to eradicate impurities and dust particles from the surface of leaves. Then the leaves were dried in air to remove water molecule before placed in a ceramic crucible. The carbonization process of tulsi leaves was carried out at 300 °C. After 3 h, a dark black carbonized product was obtained and it was allowed to cool at room temperature. The carbonized product was mechanically ground to generate fine powder. Then the powder was transferred into a 50 ml beaker and then 20 ml of double distilled water was added to it. The resultant solution was filtrated with the help of Whatman filter paper and then subjected to centrifugation to eradicate unwanted particles and to get CQD solution. This CQD solution shows blue color fluorescent under UV radiation. Similar synthesis procedure route for CQD is reported in earlier literatures [51,52]. The overall preparation procedure of CQDs is represented in the Scheme S1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of carbon quantum dots incorporated polyacrylamide/dextran (PAM/Dex/CQD) nanocomposite hydrogel
ce:section.ce:para: PAM/Dex/CQD nanocomposite hydrogel was fabricated by following the procedure mentioned below. Calculated amount of Dex (1, 2, 3 and 4 wt%) was taken in a three necked round bottom flask and was placed on a magnetic stirrer. Then 0.8 M AM was added to the Dex solution followed by the addition of 2 ml of 0.1 M MBA. The reaction system was stirred under the temperature of 30 °C for about 20 min. After that 2 ml. of 0.1 M KPS was added and then the reaction mixture was continuously purged with nitrogen gas to maintain an inert atmosphere. In the final step of synthesis procedure of PAM/Dex/CQD nanocomposite hydrogel, CQD was incorporated to the polymeric solution of PAM/Dex. Then the temperature was raised to 60 °C for 20 min. After 20 min of stirring, PAM/Dex/CQD nanocomposite hydrogel was obtained. Finally, the hydrogel was transferred into a petri-dish, which was dried in a hot air oven at 50 °C for 48 h to carry out further experimental process.

β-CD as host molecule and Cipro; an antibiotic drug as guest molecule were taken for the preparation of IC. To prepare β-CD and Cipro IC (β-Cipro IC) first equimolar solutions of β-CD and Cipro were prepared and then both the solutions were mixed and stirred for 48 h at normal temperature. Following 48 h of vigorous stirring a milky white solution was produced. The resultant solution was nothing but the 1:1 β-Cipro IC.

The photophysical behavior of CQD was investigated using Shimadzu's UV-1900i UV–Visible Spectrophotometer and Agilent-Cary Eclipse Fluorescence Spectrophotometer. Transmittance peaks of CQD, Dex, PAM/Dex hydrogel and PAM/Dex/CQD nanocomposite hydrogel were obtained by using Bruker Alpha FTIR Spectrophotometer. The samples were placed on sample holder for FTIR analysis in the range of 4000 cm−1 to 400 cm−1. Microstructural analysis of as-synthesized materials was evaluated by field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM). FESEM and HRTEM images were collected by using Gemini-SEM 450 (Zeiss) and FEI Tecnai T20 200 keV instrument, respectively. Thermal stability of the synthesized hydrogels was measured by using PerkinElmer Simultaneous Thermal Analyzer STA 8000. In this analysis the samples are heated with a constant heating rate of 10 °C/min up to 600 °C and the change in weight % is recorded. Crystallographic structure of nanocomposite was scrutinized by Rigaku X-Ray diffractometer. The cumulative drug release study was performed using Shimadzu's UV-1900i UV–Visible spectrometer.

The gel fraction (GF) or gel content of a hydrogel represents the proportion of the polymer that has effectively established a gel-like network within the substance. The dried synthesized samples were submerged in the distilled water and were again kept in the oven to dry the hydrogels. After that GF of as-synthesized composite and nanocomposite hydrogel was computed using the following formula.
(1)
GF
%
=
W di
/
W df
× 100 %
where Wdi represents the weight of the as-prepared hydrogel after drying at initial time and Wdf corresponds to the weight of dried hydrogel after eradicating soluble fraction.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Swelling study and water retention analysis
ce:section.ce:para:
The water or biological fluid uptake capacity, also known as the swelling ability of the hydrogel, was computed using a gravimetric approach. To determine the water intake percentage of the hydrogel, certain quantity of dry hydrogel was taken and allowed to swell by immersing it in different solutions. The swelling percentage of the hydrogel at a particular time was measured through the following process: initially, the hydrogel was taken out from the swelling medium and then gently wiped with soft tissue to remove excess surface water. Subsequently, the weight of the swollen hydrogel at that specific time was measured. The quantity of water absorbed by the hydrogel was measured using the following formula.
(2)
SR
%
=
W s
−
W d
/
W d
× 100 %
where SR (%) is the percentage of swelling ratio, Ws is the weight of swollen hydrogel and Wd is the weight of dried hydrogel. Finally, the hydrogel was put back to the swelling medium and the process was continued until the equilibrium swelling of hydrogel was obtained. For this particular study, swelling behavior study of fabricated hydrogels was conducted in double distilled water, acidic medium (pH 1.5), neutral buffer (pH 7.0 ± 0.05) and basic buffer (pH 9.2 ± 0.05).

The assessment of water retention property of hydrogel was carried out after the hydrogel had achieved its equilibrium swelling in double distilled water. Hydrogels at their maximum swelling capacity were placed inside the desiccator with silica gel at room temperature. At 24 h time intervals, the hydrogels were taken out of the desiccator and weighed.

The water retention % of hydrogel was calculated by using the mentioned equation
(3)
W r
%
=
W f
/
W i
× 100 %
where Wr (%) is the percentage of water retention, Wf is the weight of hydrogel after drying and Wi is the weight of hydrogel at initial state.

To investigate the biodegradability aspect, the synthesized materials were subjected to treatment with activated sludge water. The analysis was conducted at 3-day interlude across a time span of 21 days. The biodegradability of the materials was scrutinized by monitoring the weight loss relative to time. Weight percentage during the bio-disintegration of the materials was determined with the assistance of the following mathematical equation
(4)
Weight
%
=
W fd
/
W id
× 100
where Wid represents initial mass of the as-prepared hydrogel and Wfd denotes final mass of the as-synthesized hydrogel after the bio-disintegration process.

Cytotoxicity effects of PAM/Dex/CQD are determined with the help of cell viability assessment. To carry out cell viability assessment, cervical cancer (HeLa cells) and the XTT assay technique was utilized. HeLa cells were cultured in a 96 well plate with a density of 8000 cells per well in 200 μL of growth medium. Then the well plate was incubated for 24 h at 37 °C. PAM/Dex/CQD nanocomposite hydrogel was dissolved in double distilled water followed by the addition into HeLa cell wells at different concentration (0.05, 0.1, 0.5, 1, 1.5 and 2 μM) and then again incubated for another 24 h. This process was followed by treating the wells with DMSO and active XTT solution for about 8 h at 37 °C after incubation period was over. After that there was the generation of colored dye to carry out absorbance analysis and the measurements were recorded at 450 nm. The cell viability (%) was determined by utilizing the following equation
(5)
Cell viability
%
= Absorbance
at
450
nm
sample
/ Absorbance
at
450
nm
control
× 100

The β-Cipro IC loaded PAM/Dex composite and PAM/Dex/CQD nanocomposite hydrogel was prepared by immersing PAM/Dex and PAM/Dex/CQD hydrogel in 25 ml aqueous solution of β-Cipro for 28 h and 32 h, respectively to achieve their equilibrium swelling state. In addition to this for controlled drug release study, composite and nanocomposite hydrogels were loaded with only Cipro drug. After the execution drug entrapment process, drug loaded hydrogels (DLHs) were taken out and kept inside the desiccator until these were completely dry. Drug loading (DL) of hydrogels was calculated by using the following formula
(6)
DL =
W ADL
−
W BDL
/
W BDL
where, WADL and WBDL are the weight of hydrogel before and after the loading of Cipro or β-Cipro IC, respectively. The drug administration study was investigated by re-swelling the previously dried only Cipro and β-Cipro IC loaded composite hydrogel and nanocomposite hydrogel samples in acidic, neutral and basic medium. To analyze the amount of drug released at a particular interval of time, each time 3 ml of aliquots were taken out and subjected to investigation using by UV–Visible spectrophotometer. The drug release was quantified by correlating the absorbance of model drug at 272 nm with a standard calibration curve prepared for pristine drug solutions. Drug release % was calculated by using the following formula
(7)
Drug release
%
=
CM t
/
CM 0
× 100
where CMt is amount of loaded Cipro release at a particular time interval and CM0 is the loaded amount of Cipro or β-Cipro.
Statistical analysis of in vitro drug release was conducted using one-way ANOVA in OriginPro 8.5 software, where significance was defined as p < 0.05.

Kinetics release study of β-Cipro@PAM/Dex and β-Cipro@PAM/Dex/CQD nanocomposite hydrogel was evaluated using DDsolver software as Add-ins in Microsoft Excel. This software is suitable to avoid calculation errors and determine accurate kinetics release profile. Once the release profile values were calculated, the data were transferred to the Excel to run DDsolver software to obtain R2, Akaike information criterion (AIC), model selection criterion (MSC), n, m and rate constant values. Zero order kinetic, first order kinetic, Higuchi, Korsmeyer-Peppas and Peppas-Sahlin model were considered to study drug release kinetics. The best fitting model was chosen that was able to explain the drug release kinetics according to the highest value of R2 and MSC and the lowest value of AIC [53].

The following formulae are used to evaluate different models
(8)
Zero order kinetic : F =
K 0
× t
(9)
First order kinetic : F = 100 ×
1 − Exp
−
K f
× t
(10)
Higuchi : F =
K H
×
t 0.5
(11)
Korsmeyer − Peppas : F =
K KP
×
t n
(12)
Peppas − Sahlin : F =
K 1
×
t m
+
K 2
×
t 2m
where, F is fraction of drug release and t is the time. Further, K0, Kf, KH and KKP denote zero-order release rate constant, first-order release rate constant, Higuchi dissolution constant, Korsmeyer-Peppas release rate constant, respectively. In case of Peppas-Sahlin model, the first term is related to the Fickian diffusion contribution and the second part is considered case-II relaxation of release process. For this model m is release component and K1 and K2 are the release constants [54].

PAM/Dex/CQD nanocomposite hydrogel is synthesized by using APS and MBA through a low-cost green synthesis method (Scheme 1a). The interactions present within PAM/Dex/CQD nanocomposite hydrogel is schematically represented in Scheme 1b. From this scheme, we can deduce the proposed networking structure of the synthesized hydrogel. In the nanocomposite hydrogel, the PAM polymeric chains are intricately linked through covalent bonds facilitated by the cross-linker MBA. Additionally, the PAM and Dex chains are interconnected via non-covalent interactions, specifically hydrogen bonding. Ultimately, CQD are dispersed within the polymeric chain matrix and interacted via hydrogen bonding with the matrix. Further, IC is prepared by taking β-CD and Cipro with an aim to enhance the therapeutic delivery efficacy. Interaction between the β-CD and Cipro to form IC is represented in Scheme 1c.

Photo-physical behavior of CQD is investigated by UV–Visible and fluorescence spectrophotometer (Fig. S1 and S2). UV–Vis spectrum of as-synthesized CQD is represented in Fig. S1. The absorption spectrum comprises of two broad peaks at 271 nm and 329 nm with a tail extending in the visible range which is typically observed for carbon dots. The absorption peak at 271 nm is assigned to the π → π* transitions of the CC of sp2 carbon domains present in the sp3 matrix whereas the broad shoulder peak at 329 nm is designated to n → π* electronic transition of functional groups which is mainly owing to the ˃C=O of carboxylic functional groups present on the surface of CQD. Further, bright blue emission can be noticed by naked eye for aqueous solution of CQD under UV radiation (inset of Fig. S1). Similar observations for absorption peaks of CQD in this UV region are also found in previous literatures [55–57].

Fluorescence emission spectra of CQD at various excitation wavelength values starting from 290 nm to 415 nm are displayed in Fig. S2. From the net-like structure of emission spectra, it is observed that the highest emission peak is occurred at 345 nm with an intensity value of 471. It is also confirmed that CQD shows excitation-dependent emission phenomenon with variation in wavelength. The alternation in excitation wavelength leads to shift emission peaks towards higher wavelength. The fluorescence intensity study of CQD is one such case, for which, at first the emission intensity escalates and then reduces with an optimal fluorescence value under excitation wavelength. For the present study of CQD, with increment in wavelength from 290 to 345 nm, the intensity of emission spectra shows up-conversion intensity peaks while down-conversion fluorescence spectra can be seen as the excitation wavelength increases from 345 to 415 nm. Quantum confinement effect is the widely established mechanism to interpret the tunable fluorescence emission spectra (excitation dependent emission). This effect is related to the width of band gap which is identified by the size and shape of the particles. So, the broadened emission spectra from 290 to 415 nm are observed due to the variation in size of the CQD particles. Another phenomenon that attributes to the fluorescence variation is surface defects which may arises owing to the presence of oxygen containing functional groups, sp2 and sp3 hybridized carbon atoms in matrix and dangling bonds that cannot be negligible owing to the smaller size of CQD. In addition to this, red edge effect explains the fluorescence peak relying on the excitation wavelength [58–60]. Therefore, tunable emissive property of CQD is the combined effects of quantum confinement effect, surface defect and red edge effect. Similar kind of excitation dependent fluorescence emission spectra of luminescent CQD is already reported in earlier literatures [61,62]. The fluorescence property of CQD has been utilized in drug delivery applications to trace the drug during the controlled administration process [63].

The structural interactions among matrix-matrix and matrix-fillers are established by FTIR analysis in the Fig. S3. The strong peaks for CQD at 3313 cm−1 and 1633 cm−1 depict the stretching frequency of OH of hydroxyl group and >C=O of carboxyl group, respectively. Similar FTIR peaks for CQD are obtained in earlier publications. Also, previously reported literatures confirm the existence of hydroxyl and carboxyl groups in CQD [64,65]. For Dex, transmittance peaks observed at 3320 cm−1 and 1630 cm−1 are corresponding to OH stretching of hydroxyl group and >C=O stretching of carboxylic group. It may be noted that there is no direct -COOH group attached to Dex. However, the peak at 1630 cm−1 in Dex may be due to oxidized hydroxyl groups present at the surface of Dex chain that appears owing to the interaction with water molecule [66–69]. The C-O-C stretching of pyranose group of Dex shows the peak at 1152 cm−1 whereas peak at 1109 cm−1 is occurred for CO stretching at the C-4 position of glucose residue. Further, transmittance peak at 1010 cm−1 depicts the existence of chain flexibility in Dex ((1 → 6) glycosidic bonds) [70]. In FTIR spectra, the peaks for OH stretching and NH stretching of PAM/Dex matrix are appeared at 3368 cm−1 and 3196 cm−1, respectively. The distinctive peaks 1696 cm−1 and 1600 cm−1 are observed due to primary amide groups present on the PAM/Dex polymeric back bone [71–73]. The OH stretching of PAM/Dex/CQD is appeared at little lower value as compared to free -OH group. This is because of the establishment of intermolecular hydrogen bonding between hydroxyl and amide groups of PAM/Dex hydrogel. Yang et al. discussed that the addition of CQD within the matrix of PVA lead to shift wavenumber towards lower value. This is due to the -OH stretching peak is influenced by the presence or absence of hydrogen bonding, which alters the stretching frequencies of the involved OH bonds, causing the wavenumber to shift towards lower value [74]. According to the above said concept, there is the presence of CQD with in PAM/Dex polymeric matrix. Hydrogen bonding occurs during interaction between CQD and matrix is also observed in other reported literatures [75,76]. Table S1 represents all summarized bond vibrations and their corresponding transmittance peaks of CQD, Dex, PAM/Dex and PAM/Dex/CQD at different wavenumber.

The surface morphologies of PAM/Dex and PAM/Dex/CQD hydrogels are determined from the FESEM micrographs (Fig. 1(a–d)). Fig. 1a is the FESEM image for PAM/Dex composite hydrogel at a magnification of 1 μm. This FESEM image confirms the regular and dense morphology of hydrogel with presence of few cracks and crevices on the surface [77]. However, some PAM aggregates can be seen on the surface in form of irregular globular shapes [78]. After the CQD incorporation, the arrangement of polymeric chains transforms to give uniform stacked morphologies (Fig. 1b). This increases interstitial voids and results in interconnected porous structures which help to achieve promising properties for drug delivery by enhancing water intake capacity of hydrogel [79]. Fig. 1c and d demonstrate the surface morphological characteristics of PAM/Dex/CQD hydrogel at a magnification of 1 μm. Selected areas in Fig. 1b is focused in Fig. 1c and d for the clear visibility of pores and to observe the change in morphology. Also, similar explanations are proclaimed for the enhancement of surface roughness and porous structure of CQD incorporated polymeric matrix [80,81]. The elemental images and composition plot obtained from EDAX analysis of PAM/Dex/CQD nanocomposite hydrogel are displayed in Fig. 1e. Herein, weight %, and atomic % of individual elements are compared in tabular form (inset of Fig. 1e).

The results of microstructural investigations of PAM/Dex/CQD nanocomposite hydrogel are investigated via HRTEM are displayed in Fig. 2(a–f). The dispersion of CQD is identified in Fig. 2a at a magnification of 100 nm and magnified image at 50 nm is represented in Fig. 2b. It is noticed that CQD are of circular shape and are dispersed without any agglomeration [82,83]. It may be due to van der Waal's force of attraction among the particles of CQDs [84,85]. It is also presumed that the well dispersion of CQD and restriction of agglomeration is due to incorporation of CQD within the PAM/Dex polymeric matrix. The sizes of CQDs are irregular but finely distributed. The observation related to size variations of CQD is also obtained during fluorescence study where the wavelength dependent excitation peaks were appeared due to irregular size of CQD [86]. Hence, structural investigation in HRTEM analysis is in accordance with the fluorescence study of material. The distribution of particle size is represented in a histogram from where average particle size of CQD is calculated as 4.13 nm (Fig. 2c). To determine the fringes within CQD, particular areas are focused in HRTEM images as displayed in Fig. 2d. Fast Fourier transform (FFT) images obtained during calculation of lattice fringes are represented in Fig. 2e. The fringe widths are calculated as 0.13 nm and 0.09 nm for two focused fringes as depicted in Fig. 2e. The clear lattice fringes with 0.13 nm inter-planner spacing attributes to the [102] facet of graphitic carbon. Analogous inter-planner spacing value of CQD is already explained in previous literature [87]. The selected area electron diffraction (SAED) image of material is demonstrated in the Fig. 2f where crystallinity sizes are calculated (2.34 Å to 3.97 Å).

Thermal stability of as-synthesized hydrogel with and without CQD is examined by TGA analysis. The Fig. S4 displays similar degradation trends in TGA thermograph for both PAM/Dex and PAM/Dex/CQD nanocomposite hydrogels. The residual mass % of both hydrogels starting from 100 °C to 600 °C at an interval of 100 °C are also listed in the inset of the Fig. S4 in a tabular form. Both composite and nanocomposite hydrogel underwent multiple degradation steps within the temperature range of 30–600 °C. Minute difference is ascertained at the preliminary step of the degradation up to 100 °C owing to the evaporation of moisture content of both the hydrogels. At 100–175 °C small degradation is noticed due to random chain breaking of polymeric chain. The main step of pyrolysis occurred around 225–398 °C corresponds to most of the weight loss owing to the disintegration of cross linked structured of hydrogels. The thermo gravimetric curve of PAM/Dex/CQD and PAM/Dex gradually become stabilized after 398 °C up to 600 °C and there are no significant changes is observed during this step. However, in this step the degradation of nanocomposite and composite hydrogels mainly occurred due to the structural disintegration of Dex. This indicates the destruction of structural integrity of hydrogel [12]. At 600 °C after the complete degradation process, 22.18 % and 27.27 % weight of initial mass are left for PAM/Dex and PAM/Dex/CQD, respectively. From the TGA data exploration, it can be concluded that the integration of CQD with the matrix of PAM/Dex enhances thermal stability of nanocomposite hydrogel by reducing the exhaustion of mass as compare to pristine hydrogel. Analogous thermal stability trends of hydrogel with and without CQD are already reported in previous literatures [80,88].

The XRD analysis determines the crystalline nature of as-synthesized PAM/Dex/CQD nanocomposite hydrogel and confirms the incorporation of CQD within the matrix of PAM/Dex. Fig. S5 is the demonstration of the XRD pattern of PAM/Dex/CQD and the inset represents the XRD pattern of CQD. The experiment to analyze XRD pattern of PAM/Dex/CQD was conducted over an angular range from 5° to 80°. From the XRD diffraction pattern, it is seen that a distinct peak at 2θ value of 22.75° is observed for PAM/Dex/CQD. From the previously reported literatures it is found that diffraction peaks for PAM and Dex are observed at 21.09° and at around 17°, respectively [89,90]. Further, the inset represented in Fig. S5 has diffraction peak at 22.8° for CQD [51]. Therefore, in accordance with the previous reports and present data, a broad diffraction peak is observed at 22.75° depicting the appropriate interaction among the matrix and filler. In addition to, the diffractogram represented in Fig. S5 also denotes the amorphous characteristics of PAM/Dex/CQD nanocomposite hydrogel. XRD method is generally implemented for the identification of d-spacing with Bragg's equation i.e., nλ = 2d sinθ. In this equation, λ (0.15406 nm) denotes to the wavelength of X-ray radiation used for the analysis of diffraction pattern, d is the spacing between the particular diffraction lattice plane and θ corresponds to the diffraction angle [91]. d-Spacing for the prepared nanocomposite hydrogel is found to be 3.9061 Å. The calculated d-spacing value is in conformity with the measured values from the SAED of HRTEM analysis which was in range of 2.34 Å to 3.97 Å. Furthermore, XRD pattern is used to measure the average crystallite size of the material by putting values in Debye Scherrer equation (τ = Kλ/βcosθ). In the previous equation τ represents the crystallite size, K demonstrates the dimensional shape factors (0.94) and β denotes the full width half of the maximum intensity (FWHM) [92]. By using the Debye Scherrer equation, the crystallite size of PAM/Dex/CQD is found to be 0.359 nm.

GF % of the synthesized PAM/Dex composite and PAM/Dex/CQD nanocomposite hydrogel with different amount of CQD (1 ml, 2 ml and 3 ml) is investigated. Fig. S6 represents the results obtained from the GF measurement of composite and nanocomposite hydrogel as a function of GF % and amount of CQD taken. From the GF analysis graph, it is seen that with the addition of CQD, GF % of the nanocomposite decreases due to non-covalent interaction between the CQD and polymeric matrix [93].

The comparative swelling behavior study of PAM/Dex and PAM/Dex/CQD nanocomposite hydrogel are conducted in double distilled water. The swelling behavior graphs those are plotted as time against % swelling (Fig. 3a), shows that the nanocomposite hydrogel exhibits a higher swelling ratio in swelling medium as compared to the pristine hydrogel. Also, from Fig. 3a, it is seen that the swelling percentage of PAM/Dex/CQD is 863 %, which is higher compared to the 678 % swelling percentage of PAM/Dex. The increased swelling % of the nanocomposite hydrogel is attributed to the slight lowering of GF % (Fig. S6). Moreover, formation of stacked like structures in FESEM images creates more voids which assists the higher water uptake capacity. Further, the presence of CQD within the nanocomposite hydrogel's matrix, assigned to the interactions between CQD and the polymers as mentioned in earlier literature, leads to the enhancement of the swelling percentage [94]. PAM/Dex/CQD and PAM/Dex reach equilibrium swelling after 32 h and 28 h, respectively. Also, it is noticeable that there is a decrease in the swelling ratio after reaching equilibrium swelling. Further, the swelling behavior of PAM/Dex/CQD nanocomposite hydrogel is analyzed in acidic medium (pH 1.5), neutral buffer (pH 7.0 ± 0.05) and basic buffer (pH 9.2 ± 0.05) and while comparing the graph in Fig. 3b, it becomes apparent that the swelling ratio of the hydrogel is greater in a basic medium as compare to acidic medium. The lower swelling ratio of the hydrogel in acidic medium can be elucidated by the protonation of hydrophilic functional groups i.e. –NH2 and –OH within the hydrogel structure in the presence of H+ ions [95]. This protonation restricts the construction of hydrogen bonds with water molecules. Conversely, in a basic medium, the functional groups tend to release H+ ions, resulting in a negative charge on the functional groups. This increased repulsive force between similarly charged functional groups that causes the polymeric chains to expand, maintaining stability and creating a more spacious surface for water absorption within the matrix. This expansion enhances the possibility of establishing hydrogen bonds with water molecules and increases the swelling ratio percentage. Similar pH dependent swelling behavior of hydrogel is reported in previous literatures [96–98].

The water confinement capacity of PAM/Dex and PAM/Dex/CQD nanocomposite hydrogel is analyzed in the Fig. 3c. Water retention property of a particular hydrogel depends upon various factors like extend of cross-linking density, size of pore, surface morphology and most importantly interfacial interaction among the constitute components [99]. In this study, water retention characteristics of the as-synthesized composite and nanocomposite hydrogel are investigated as a function of time (h) at room temperature for seven days. From the Fig. 3c, it is concluded that the water holding capacity of PAM/Dex/CQD hydrogel is more as compared to pristine PAM/Dex hydrogel. After seven days, the water holding capacity of PAM/Dex/CQD is measured as 16.3 % whereas for PAM/Dex it is calculated as 10.17 %. The higher water holding capacity of nanocomposite is because of the existence of a smaller number of interconnected pour structures which enhances the water absorption capacity, whereas in case of PAM/Dex hydrogel higher number of free spaces are available due to higher number of cross-linking sites. Earlier reported articles also mentioned the higher water retention property due to high cross-linking density which allow hydrogel film's configuration to lose water slightly [96]. Also, the surface roughness is enhanced due the incorporation of CQDs within the matrix of PAM/Dex as seen from FESEM images help in increasing water retention capacity. It is concluded that because of improved water holding capacity of nanocomposite hydrogel for prolonged period of time, it can be implemented as potential candidate for the controlled drug administration applications [97].

Biodegradability of the as-synthesized materials is studied by treating the materials with activated sludge water (Fig. S7). Process of biodegradability of hydrogels shows continuous decrease in weight with increase in time duration. The degradation occurs due to the presence of microbes like fungi, bacteria, yeast, etc., in sludge water. All the hydrogels show effective weight with passage of time, which can be attributed to the use of biodegradable constituents. However, it is seen that addition of CQD with the polymeric network of PAM/Dex slowers the degradation rate and PAM/Dex/CQD shows lower weight % as compare to pristine PAM/Dex. This may be due to the high antibacterial properties of CQD that prevent the hydrogel from degradation caused by bacterial action [100,101]. Similar studies have shown the slower degradability of polymer nanocomposite hydrogels as compare to pristine hydrogel after the addition of nanoparticles [102,103].

To verify the biocompatible nature and cytotoxicity effect of PAM/Dex/CQD, cell viability of the material is investigated. The cytotoxicity behavior of the nanocomposite hydrogel is carried out via XTT assay. The cell viability % of HeLa cells as a function of PAM/Dex/CQD nanocomposite's concentration is represented in Fig. 3d. 0, 0.05, 0.1, 0.5, 1, 1.5 and 2 μM concentration of PAM/Dex/CQD are taken for the cytotoxicity analysis. From the Fig. 3d, it is noticed that there is gradual reduction in cell viability % of HeLa cells as the concentration of nanocomposite hydrogel increases starting from 0.05 μM to 2 μM. At concentration of 2 μM, HeLa cells showed 78 % of cell viability while at 0.05 μM of concentration the cell viability was 95 %.

pH responsive comparative drug administration study for Cipro loaded PAM/Dex and PAM/Dex/CQD nanocomposite hydrogel is carried out using UV–Visible spectroscopy. Upon the penetration of the dissolution medium into the polymeric matrix, drug molecules started to diffuse into the surrounding environment and then an aliquot was taken to analysis drug release %. The Fig. 4(a–c) represents the Cipro release profile from Cipro@PAM/Dex/CQD and Cipro@PAM/Dex in three different pH media (1.5, 7.0 ± 0.05 and 9.2 ± 0.05) in room temperature. The insets of Fig. 4(a–c) consist of release profile graph for first 6 h of Cipro release. The amount of drug released from nanocomposite hydrogel is found to be greater than composite hydrogel. This can be explained by considering the higher swelling percentage and surface morphology of PAM/Dex/CQD nanocomposite hydrogel that help in faster release of model drug. Further, the drug release profile shows high release % in acidic medium than basic and neutral medium. In acidic medium the release % is 52.37 and 47.92 for PAM/Dex/CQD and PAM/Dex hydrogel, respectively in 24 h while for the same period of time the release profile showed drug administration values of 41.96 % and 37.90 % in neutral medium and 41.75 % and 39.52 % in basic medium for nanocomposite and composite hydrogel. Moreover, the equilibrium drug release % is displayed in the Fig. 4d for both hydrogels that showed highest drug release % for Cipro@PAM/Dex/CQD in acidic medium. The variation in Cipro release phenomenon for hydrogels in different medium can be described according to the value of pH [104]. Although the swelling ratio % of hydrogels are more in basic medium, but, the drug release % is higher in acidic medium. The higher release % of model drug can be elucidated on the basis of the pH dependent solubility of Cipro as the extent of solubility of Cipro is reduced within the pH range of 5 to 9 and has higher solubility in acidic pH [45]. This pH dependent solubility also indicates the approximately similar Cipro release profile in neutral and basic medium. The present findings conclude that the as-synthesized hydrogels those of pH responsive materials can be employed as one of the potential candidates in drug administration process since they could be helpful in enhancing drug release % at requisite site and prevent unnecessary deterioration of human physiological condition due to intake of high drug dosages.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro drug release study of β-Cipro@PAM/Dex/CQD and β-Cipro@PAM/Dex
ce:section.ce:para: Similar to the control drug release experiment, the comparative administration profiles of drug from β-Cipro@PAM/Dex/CQD and β-Cipro@PAM/Dex hydrogels at room temperature in three different pH conditions i.e., pH 1.5, 7.0 ± 0.05 and 9.2 ± 0.05 are represented in Fig. 5 (a-c) as a function of time. The insets of Fig. 5(a–c) are the demonstration of drug release profile from PAM/Dex and PAM/Dex/CQD nanocomposite hydrogel for the first 6 h. It is observed that the drug administration profiles of both β-Cipro loaded hydrogels are higher in acidic medium. In addition to this, it is also seen that in all three cases, release profile is more for PAM/Dex/CQD nanocomposite hydrogel which can be associated with the higher swelling ratio of nanocomposite hydrogel. Within a period of 24 h, 54.32 %, 32.85 % and 30.07 % of loaded drug are released from β-Cipro@PAM/Dex/CQD nanocomposite hydrogel for acidic, neutral and basic medium, respectively while for β-Cipro@PAM/Dex hydrogel the % of drug release are 47.9, 31.15 and 25.47. Fig. 5d displays the drug release % of nanocomposite and composite hydrogel at their respective equilibrium swelling time. In acidic medium, drug release % of Cipro from β-Cipro@PAM/Dex/CQD and β-Cipro@PAM/Dex hydrogels are found to be 64.15 % and 49.41 % in 32 and 28 h, respectively whereas in case of basic medium the release profiles show 42.26 % and 38.43 % of drug release. The higher release rate of Cipro in acidic medium can be explained as discussed in the previous section. Also, β-CD based Cipro drug delivery system show similar kind of drug release profile in acidic medium [105]. Therefore, the developed nanocomposite hydrogel can be implemented for the administration of Cipro drug in stomach. Moreover, the formation of IC complex has significant impacts not only in drug loading process but also in release of Cipro from the synthesized hydrogel which is clearly evident from the drug release profiles for β-Cipro loaded hydrogels. It is also confirmed that drug release through β-Cipro loaded hydrogel is sustained as-compared to only Cipro loaded hydrogel. Therefore, the findings from this study offer valuable insights to advance the development of novel polymeric matrices incorporating β-Cipro IC for extended drug release. Analogous comparative drug release study for with and without β-CD based therapeutic administration vehicles are reported in many literatures [45,51,106,107]. The incorporation of IC within the matrix of hydrogel can lead to controlled and sustainable drug release which is mainly due to dual mechanism; one is swelling of hydrogels and other one is the formation of IC through host-guest interaction between drug and β-CD [45]. The interaction between Cipro and β-CD is attributed to hydrogen bonding, dipole interactions, hydrophobic interactions, and van der Waal's forces of attraction [47,108]. Some comparative studies of drug release percentage as a functional of time (h) with earlier literatures based on β-CD associated drug delivery vehicles are demonstrated in Table 1 .

The one-way ANOVA analysis is based on the mean ± SD and statistically significant is calculated by considering the p value < 0.05. From the statistical analysis, the p values estimated from the mean ± SD data for β-Cipro@PAM/Dex/CQD nanocomposite hydrogel in acidic medium is found to be <0.05. This indicates the statistical significance of the obtained results. Herein, statistical analysis validates the experimental results of the in vitro drugs release study.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Kinetics of in vitro drug release study
ce:section.ce:para: The analysis of kinetics drug release profile provides comparative mathematical investigation of drug administration from swellable polymeric drug transportation vehicle to find the most fitted model applicable to multi mechanism release. Further, mathematical modeling of drug administration from a drug carrying vehicle is frequently employed to recognize the primary factors influencing the rate of drug release from a carrier. The ultimate objective of the kinetic study is to identify the optimal conditions that help in describing in vivo release profile. Hence, predication of in vivo performance of therapeutics with the help of in vitro drug release profile is considered as the rational development of the controlled release drug administration vehicles [118]. The most suitable kinetic mechanisms that help in regulating controlled release profile of a drug are diffusion, swelling and erosion [54]. However, diffusion-controlled release is the most frequently applicable mechanism utilized to characterized drug administration process from hydrogel [119]. In agreement with the Fick's law, either constants or variable diffusion coefficient is the important parameter to discuss diffusion-controlled release. The kinetic study of drug release is carried out using various established mathematical formula to certify the appropriate mechanism of drug release. Furthermore, the release mechanism helps to interpret drug administration related relationship from various kinetic drug release profiles [120]. In this present experiment, kinetics of drug dissolution profile is investigated using DDsolver software. The calculated data obtained for R2, AIC, MSC, n, m and all the kinetic model related rate constants values from DDsolver software for zero order, first order, Higuchi, Korsmeyer-Peppas and Peppas-Sahlin model are represented in the Table 2 . The plots obtained from DDsolver software during the investigation of kinetics drug relase profile of β-Cipro@PAM/Dex and β-Cipro@PAM/Dex/CQD in acidic, neutral and basic medium using Korsmeyer-Peppas and Peppas-Sahlin model are represented as Fig. S8 and Fig. S9. In case of Korsmeyer-Peppas model, different n values denote different types of diffusion mechanism. When n value is <0.5, the therapeutic release is considered as Fickian type diffusion whereas; if n value is >0.5 and <1, the release is influenced by non-Fickian mechanism. However, when n is equal to 1, the polymer chain relaxation is the reason behind the drug release [121]. As from the kinetic release profile tabulated in Table 2, it is seen that the exponent n value for β-Cipro loaded PAM/Dex/CQD nanocomposite and PAM/Dex hydrogels in acidic medium are 0.428 and 0.398, respectively. Therefore, for both the hydrogels, the release process follows Fickian diffusion mechanism in acidic medium. However, in basic and neutral medium the release process follows non-Fickian diffusion process. Moreover, it is seen that Peppas-Sahlin is most suitable model among the five kinetic models describe in this section for β-Cipro@PAM/Dex/CQD nanocomposite hydrogel in acidic medium according to R2 value (0.9884). This model is based on the two additive mechanisms one is Fickian diffusion and another one is case-II relaxation [54]. The values of constant K2 those correspond to the polymer relaxation mechanism are less in comparison to the values of K1. Therefore, the release of drug from β-Cipro@PAM/Dex/CQD hydrogel in acidic medium is likely to be compatible with Fickian diffusion which is also explained through Korsmeyer-Peppas model.

Photoluminescent CQDs with average size of 4.13 nm are synthesized to be incorporated within PAM/Dex matrix. The resulting material is found to be non-toxic from the cytotoxicity assay of PAM/Dex/CQD on HeLa cells. β-CD successfully interacts with Cipro to form IC which enables safe encapsulation and release of the drug via host-guest mechanism. β-Cipro@PAM/Dex/CQD exhibited higher drug uptake as compared to Cipro@PAM/Dex/CQD and delayed Cipro release of about 64.15 % in 32 h in acidic medium. The higher drug release tendency of the synthesized nanocomposite hydrogel in acidic medium as compared to the basic and neutral environments, strongly suggests the use of β-Cipro@PAM/Dex/CQD hydrogels for stomach-targeted drug release. Moreover, the delayed release response of the hydrogel for a period of 32 h ensures the prolonged bioavailability of Cipro in human body which may solve the issue of repeated drug administration. Therefore, the challenge of the present study is to maintain the pH medium to ensure prolonged and controlled release. Release rate constant, R2, AIC and MSC are calculated theoretically by using DDsolver software and the outcomes support Fickian diffusion mechanism for drug administration in acidic medium. Hence, the fabricated β-Cipro@PAM/Dex/CQD nanocomposite hydrogel is a potential material as a vehicle for sustainable release of drugs with assistance of IC with β-CD. In addition, the study of in vitro release of Cipro by β-CD assisted PAM/Dex/CQD polymeric system may motivate for in vivo investigation of drug release rate using animal model.

Krishna Manjari Sahu: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation. Anuradha Biswal: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Formal analysis. Upuluri Manisha: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Formal analysis. Sarat K. Swain: Validation, Supervision, Investigation, Formal analysis, Conceptualization.
